changelog.summary: Initial release. 24 questions, 6 categories, 4 archetype profiles, Alex Mercer persona, incumbent rebid modifier.

Pursuit Viability redesign. 25 v6 questions, 5 categories, 8 archetype profiles, 4-stage model, 5-band RAG tier output, challenger overlay added, incumbent overlay restructured to 8 questions. PWIN number suppressed; viability tier is headline output.

stages.label: Identify
stages.description: The opportunity has been scanned and an initial view formed. Pre-market or earliest market-engagement signals have been picked up. No formal commitment to pursue has been made. Viability at this stage is mostly about strategic fit, preliminary contestability, and whether the opportunity is worth investing time to understand more deeply. Thin evidence bases are normal and expected. Strong assessments are rare by design -- a Strong at Identify requires genuine structural advantage that does not need further validation, not just early optimism.

stages.label: Capture
stages.description: Active capture is underway. The team is engaging with the buyer ahead of any formal ITT or RFP. This is where relationship intelligence, buyer understanding, stakeholder access, and competitive position are built. Viability is mostly about whether the team is on the right trajectory -- gaps at Capture are recoverable if there is time to close them, but structural weaknesses (no champion, no insight, no differentiated position) become very difficult to fix once the ITT lands. Assessments should reflect what the team actually knows, not what they intend to find out.

stages.label: Pursue
stages.description: The ITT or RFP is live. A formal pursuit is underway and the clock is running. Evidence depth, scoreability of the proposition, delivery confidence, and commercial viability all matter at this stage. The tolerance for gaps is much lower than at Capture -- what is not known or not built now will be absent from the submission. Assessments should be honest about what remains unresolved, because at Pursue, a gap is no longer a future action but a current risk.

stages.label: Submit
stages.description: The submission has been made or is imminent. The team may be at shortlist, BAFO, or preferred bidder stage. The question is about submission strength, competitive defensibility, and late-stage risk rather than evidence-building. Viability at Submit reflects the quality of what was built and written, not what is still intended. A Walk Away at Submit does not mean stop -- it means the expected value of continued investment is low and the team should be clear-eyed about why.

ragTiers.label: Strong
ragTiers.oneLineMeaning: Viability is high for the current stage. Position is earned and evidenced. Continue and consolidate.

ragTiers.label: On Track
ragTiers.oneLineMeaning: Viability is appropriate for the current stage. Some gaps but nothing structural. Continue with focused improvements.

ragTiers.label: Conditional
ragTiers.oneLineMeaning: Viability is at the lower edge of acceptable for the current stage. Specific conditions must be met before further investment is justified.

ragTiers.label: Major Concerns
ragTiers.oneLineMeaning: Viability is below what the stage requires. Material reshaping is required, or the pursuit should move to watch/shape rather than active pursuit.

ragTiers.label: Walk Away
ragTiers.oneLineMeaning: Viability is structurally weak. Continued investment is not justified. Recommend qualifying out or returning to early-stage shaping only if the underlying conditions change.

categories.name: Buyer Need & Decision Logic
categories.description: Tests the depth and currency of the team's understanding of why the buyer is going to market, what they most need, and what would make them choose change over continuity.

categories.name: Stakeholder Position & Access
categories.description: Tests whether the team knows who really shapes the decision, has access to or reliable insight into key stakeholders, and understands what could work against them from a people and politics perspective.

categories.name: Competitive Position & Incumbent Dynamics
categories.description: Tests whether the team understands the competitive field well enough to win in it -- identifying the likely winner, understanding incumbent defensibility, and demonstrating an evidenced advantage where it matters most.

categories.name: Procurement, Proposition & Solution Fit
categories.description: Tests whether the procurement design can reward the team's strengths, whether eligibility and suitability criteria are met, and whether the proposition and proof story are aligned to what the buyer actually values.

categories.name: Commercial, Delivery & Pursuit Readiness
categories.description: Tests whether the deal is commercially viable, the delivery model is credible at the required scale, risks are understood and manageable, and the pursuit team has the capacity to execute to the required standard.

consulting_advisory.name: Professional Consulting / Advisory
consulting_advisory.rationale: People, reputation, problem understanding, stakeholder pull, and case study relevance dominate.

digital_transformation_si.name: Digital Transformation / SI / Delivery
digital_transformation_si.rationale: Solution fit, delivery credibility, and scoreability matter as much as stakeholder access.

digital_product_saas.name: Digital Product / SaaS / Platform
digital_product_saas.rationale: Product / roadmap fit, technical compliance, integration, and commercial model are decisive.

bpo_managed_service.name: BPO / Managed Service / Operational Outsourcing
bpo_managed_service.rationale: Operational continuity, transition, pricing, and delivery credibility dominate.

facilities_operational.name: Facilities / Field / Operational Service
facilities_operational.rationale: Mobilisation, staffing, operational resilience, and commercial delivery realism dominate.

hybrid_transformation_managed.name: Hybrid Transformation + Managed Service
hybrid_transformation_managed.rationale: Balanced model where proposition fit and delivery credibility both matter.

framework_panel.name: Framework / Panel Appointment
framework_panel.rationale: Baseline compliance, breadth of capability, references, and commercial construct dominate.

incumbent_renewal.name: Incumbent Renewal / Rebid
incumbent_renewal.rationale: Retention logic, switching risk, value-for-money defence, and renewal-plus-improvement matter most.

core.name: Core 25 Questions
core.description: The standard 25-question viability model. Applied to all pursuits. Archetype-adjusted category weights rebalance the score without changing the questions.

questions.text: There is a clearly evidenced reason the buyer wants meaningful change now.
questions.whyAsked: Tests the strength of the buyer's need-to-change rather than assuming the existence of a tender means appetite for change.

questions.benchmarkScenario: S1 -- Central government | BPO managed service | £100m | Capture phase | Challenger | Reprocurement from incumbent

benchmarkExamples.Strongly Agree: Buyer Decision Thesis records three change triggers for the department: current service KPIs have slipped for two consecutive quarters, the operating cost base exceeds the business case target, and the sponsor wants a lower-risk transition before the next spending review. These points are triangulated through the market-engagement pack, account notes, and the Opportunity Brief, and the implication documented is that the buyer wants measurable service improvement rather than another like-for-like renewal.
benchmarkExamples.Agree: The Opportunity Brief states that the department is seeking service improvement and cost reduction, and account intelligence suggests current performance is under review. The team has documented a credible reason for change, but the evidence is still partly indirect and not yet fully translated into a clear buyer decision thesis.
benchmarkExamples.Disagree: The team assumes the department wants change because the contract is being recompeted and because efficiency is a common theme in central government. The note names no decisive trigger and does not document why the buyer would want meaningful movement rather than a routine rerun.
benchmarkExamples.Strongly Disagree: We think the buyer wants change because there is a tender. No documented trigger, stakeholder signal, or service issue is recorded to support that conclusion.

challengeQuestionsByLevel.Strongly Agree: Re-test whether the documented need-to-change is still current, whether the strongest evidence comes from direct buyer signals or inference, and whether the decision thesis would survive a challenge from procurement, finance, and operations.
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Agree: Which part of the buyer need-to-change is still inferred, weakly validated, or not yet translated into a clear decision thesis? What conflicting evidence exists?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Disagree: Have we confused a published procurement with real buyer appetite for change? What decisive trigger, consequence of inaction, or stakeholder priority is still missing?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Strongly Disagree: What evidence would we need before we could credibly claim real buyer momentum? Is this pursuit being carried by hope rather than need?

Use A01 Opportunity Brief; A02 Buyer Decision Thesis; A05 Buyer Anxiety & Constraint Map; A22 Buyer Engagement & Signal Log to document the missing decision, trade-off, and implication; then test the conclusion with fresher buyer / market evidence.

Before scoring positively, rebuild the relevant artifact outcome(s) in A01 Opportunity Brief; A02 Buyer Decision Thesis; A05 Buyer Anxiety & Constraint Map; A22 Buyer Engagement & Signal Log, gather direct evidence, and decide whether the pursuit should be reshaped, deferred, or stopped for [{Stage}] / [{Role}] / [{Deal Size}].

questions.benchmarkScenario: S1 -- Central government | BPO managed service | £100m | Capture phase | Challenger | Reprocurement from incumbent

benchmarkExamples.Strongly Agree: The Buyer Decision Thesis documents that the procurement is tied to a priority operating issue: service failures are hitting ministerial attention, the programme is on the departmental risk register, and the sponsor has linked the outcome to a formal efficiency target. The team has written down why this is a live business problem rather than a procedural buying event and what that means for our positioning.
benchmarkExamples.Agree: The team has recorded that the opportunity appears important because of cost pressure and service expectations, and there are signs the sponsor cares about the outcome. The note is credible, but it does not yet prove the problem sits high enough on the buyer agenda to outweigh continuity bias.
benchmarkExamples.Disagree: The write-up says this looks important because of the contract value and the visibility of the department. It does not evidence that the problem is priority-led rather than simply a scheduled procurement.
benchmarkExamples.Strongly Disagree: There is no written basis for saying this is a high-priority problem; the team is treating the opportunity as important simply because it exists.

questions.benchmarkScenario: S2 -- Local government | Consulting / advisory | £3m | Pre-gate | Challenger | Open competition

benchmarkExamples.Strongly Agree: The Buyer Decision Thesis for the council sets out the top three outcomes as faster decision support to senior leaders, a politically credible transformation roadmap, and capability transfer to internal teams. It also records the trade-off that pace matters more than broad scope in year one, which is why the solution spine is deliberately narrow and people-led.
benchmarkExamples.Agree: The team has identified likely council outcomes around transformation, assurance, and pace, and those priorities are referenced in the working narrative. However, the trade-offs between them are not yet fully evidenced or consistently reflected across the artifacts.
benchmarkExamples.Disagree: The note lists several plausible outcomes for the council but does not clearly rank them or explain what the buyer is willing to sacrifice. It reads more like a generic consulting proposition than a documented decision view.
benchmarkExamples.Strongly Disagree: No clear top outcomes or trade-offs are documented; the response relies on broad assumptions about what councils usually want.

questions.benchmarkScenario: S1 -- Central government | BPO managed service | £100m | Capture phase | Challenger | Reprocurement from incumbent

benchmarkExamples.Strongly Agree: The team has documented that the department may retain the incumbent because of operational embeddedness, lower perceived transition risk, and acceptable baseline performance in two critical service towers. That conclusion is captured in the Incumbent Defensibility Assessment together with the implication that our case must make switching risk feel lower than staying put.
benchmarkExamples.Agree: The team has written down continuity, embedded knowledge, and buyer caution as likely reasons to stay with the incumbent. The logic is credible, but it is not yet supported by enough direct evidence on how heavily those factors will influence the decision.
benchmarkExamples.Disagree: The note says the incumbent will probably be favored because incumbents usually are. It does not document the specific retention drivers for this buyer or the implications for the pursuit.

incumbent.name: Incumbent Position Modifier
incumbent.description: Activates when "We are the incumbent defending this contract" is selected. Adds 12 rebid-specific questions as a 7th category, additional persona triggers for incumbent inflation patterns, and a separate Rebid Risk Assessment AI output alongside the standard PWIN report.

matches: We are the incumbent defending this contract

designPrinciples.principle: The new business framework will systematically over-score incumbent positions.
designPrinciples.rationale: The current 24 questions ask 'have you built X?' An incumbent answers yes — they have built relationships, they have delivery intelligence, they have a proposition. But the question is wrong for a rebid. The right question is 'has X decayed without you noticing?' These are structurally different assessments requiring structurally different questions.

designPrinciples.principle: Incumbents suffer from complacency blindness — not dishonesty.
designPrinciples.rationale: A pursuit team that has delivered a contract for 5 years genuinely believes their relationships are strong, their performance is valued, and their position is secure. They are frequently wrong. The rebid module is designed to surface the gaps in that belief system, not to challenge the team's integrity. Alex's tone for rebids is diagnostic, not adversarial.

designPrinciples.principle: The most dangerous rebid position is the one that feels safest.
designPrinciples.rationale: A team with high confidence in their incumbent position is more vulnerable than a team with healthy uncertainty. The rebid module specifically probes the assumptions that underpin high confidence — the contacts who haven't been honest, the political map that hasn't been updated, the value proposition the client no longer describes in our terms.

designPrinciples.principle: The rebid question is not 'will we win?' — it is 'what do our competitors know that we don't?'
designPrinciples.rationale: This reframes the output of the rebid assessment from a win probability score to a vulnerability intelligence report. The Rebid Risk Assessment answers a different question than the standard PWIN report — and that different question is worth considerably more to the client.

designPrinciples.principle: Architecture: modifier layer, not replacement framework.
designPrinciples.rationale: The 12 rebid questions activate alongside the standard 24 when the incumbent context is selected. The PWIN score reflects both layers. The Rebid Risk Assessment is a separate output that sits alongside the standard PWIN report. This preserves comparability while adding the incumbent-specific intelligence.

architecture.detail: Activates when Pursuit Context form shows 'We are the incumbent defending this contract'. The 12 rebid questions appear as a new section at the end of the Assessment tab, clearly labelled 'Incumbent Position Assessment'. The standard 24 questions remain unchanged.

The 12 rebid questions use the same 4-point scale as the standard questions. They form a seventh category: 'Incumbent Position' — weighted at 20% of the total PWIN when active, with the other six categories reweighted proportionally downward. This reflects the fundamental importance of incumbent position health in rebid outcomes.

An incumbent who scores poorly on the rebid module will see their overall PWIN reduced even if their standard 24-question scores are high. This is the correct behaviour — a team with strong pursuit mechanics but a decaying incumbent position is in genuine danger.

In addition to the standard PWIN report, the AI generates a Rebid Risk Assessment — a distinct document that identifies the top vulnerability areas, estimates challenger exploit risk for each, and recommends a strategic posture: Defend and Shape / Active Recovery / Emergency Intervention / Qualify Out.

When the rebid module is active, Alex's system prompt receives additional rebid-specific calibration: heightened scepticism on relationship claims, specific inflation triggers for incumbent language patterns, and instructions to probe the four decay risk areas in every challenge question set.

addsQuestions.text: We have clear evidence of why the buyer would retain us beyond simple continuity bias.
addsQuestions.especiallyCriticalFor: Use opportunity archetype, stage, role, and deal size to judge how heavily to weight this in context.
addsQuestions.whyAsked: Tests whether retention rests on positive value, not inertia alone.
addsQuestions.whyItMatters: The most common rebid blind spot is the gap between how the delivery team reads the relationship and how senior client stakeholders actually rate the contract. Operational feedback flows continuously, but strategic dissatisfaction surfaces in governance meetings the incumbent does not attend — a client scoring the contract acceptable operationally may simultaneously be telling their Board it is underperforming strategically. Beyond the perception gap, the quality of an internal champion matters as much as their existence: a contact who is personally comfortable with the relationship and avoids difficult conversations is not a champion but a friendly presence. The real test is whether that individual has taken visible political risk to defend the incumbent in internal discussions where the incumbent was not in the room. Retention built on genuine advocacy from a tested champion is durable; retention built on relationship comfort is a single resignation away from collapse.
addsQuestions.executionEvidence: State the positive reasons for retention and show why the buyer sees continuing with us as attractive, not merely safer.

addsQuestions.especiallyCriticalFor: Use opportunity archetype, stage, role, and deal size to judge how heavily to weight this in context.
addsQuestions.whyAsked: Forces honesty about renewal risk and dissatisfaction.
addsQuestions.whyItMatters: A challenger who has done proper competitive intelligence will know more about the incumbent's performance weaknesses than the incumbent's own pursuit team — because they will have obtained that intelligence from client contacts who feel safe being honest with someone outside the relationship. At the same time, the client's value narrative evolves over a contract term while the incumbent's proposition often calcifies around what was important at mobilisation. A client whose priorities have shifted will find a challenger's framing more relevant than the incumbent's, even if the incumbent is delivering more. The incumbent must therefore apply the same discipline to understanding its own vulnerabilities — both performance and value-perception — that a well-prepared challenger applies to its competitor analysis. If you cannot name the specific push factors that might drive the client to change, you are not ready to neutralise them.

Delivery teams receive operational feedback continuously but rarely hear senior-level strategic dissatisfaction directly. The incumbent must therefore apply the same discipline to its own performance record that a challenger applies to a competitor: name the three things a challenger will attack you on, document the narrative that addresses each, and test that narrative with at least one trusted client contact before the ITT window opens. addsQuestions.text: We have a credible renewal-plus-improvement story, not just a continuity story.
addsQuestions.especiallyCriticalFor: Use opportunity archetype, stage, role, and deal size to judge how heavily to weight this in context.
addsQuestions.whyAsked: Tests whether the incumbent offer includes future value.
addsQuestions.whyItMatters: Over a long contract the incumbent's value narrative calcifies around what was important at mobilisation, while the client's priorities evolve. A challenger presenting in the client's current language will feel more relevant than the incumbent even if the incumbent is delivering more. The most credible counter to this is a renewal-plus-improvement proposition that has already happened — a hire made, a system procured, a partnership formed — not one promised in the submission. An incumbent whose rebid proposition looks identical to the current service has implicitly told the client that the next contract will be the same as the last one, and a challenger can credibly promise transformation precisely because they have no legacy to defend. Visible, pre-committed investment in the future signals that the incumbent is competing, not defending.
addsQuestions.executionEvidence: Set out how renewal improves outcomes, value, or capability rather than simply extending the status quo.

addsQuestions.text: The buyer recognizes the transition, operational, knowledge, or service risk of switching away from us.
addsQuestions.especiallyCriticalFor: Use opportunity archetype, stage, role, and deal size to judge how heavily to weight this in context.
addsQuestions.whyAsked: Tests whether switching risk is visible to the buyer.
addsQuestions.whyItMatters: Transition risk is the incumbent's most powerful competitive weapon and the most frequently wasted. Generic statements about disruption risk and knowledge transfer are ignored by experienced evaluators. Specific, quantified, credible transition risk analysis — built in the client's own risk framework, validated with their risk function, and evidenced by comparable transitions in the market — is very difficult for a challenger to rebut. The test is not whether the incumbent can articulate the risk internally; it is whether the client's risk and finance functions have engaged with it and acknowledged its materiality. A client who understands specifically what they would be taking on by changing provider needs a compelling reason to change; a client who has never been shown that analysis is only constrained by their own inertia.
addsQuestions.executionEvidence: Show evidence that the buyer appreciates the disruption or risk of change, rather than assuming they will infer it.

addsQuestions.text: We are not overexposed on price benchmarking, complacency, or relationship fatigue.
addsQuestions.especiallyCriticalFor: Use opportunity archetype, stage, role, and deal size to judge how heavily to weight this in context.
addsQuestions.whyItMatters: Incumbent relationships are maintained through delivery. Pursuit relationships require a different kind of investment — conversations about the future, about the client's priorities for the next contract term, about what a new contract should look like. A team with excellent operational relationships may have zero pursuit relationships if they have never had that conversation explicitly with senior decision-makers. Personnel change constantly in public sector clients, and an incumbent operating from a political map built at contract mobilisation is navigating a landscape that no longer exists. New SROs bring new priorities; restructuring creates new power centres. A challenger building their political map today has a more accurate picture of current client dynamics than an incumbent who has not actively maintained theirs — and this is one of the most frequently exploited incumbent vulnerabilities.
addsQuestions.executionEvidence: Show that price reset, complacency risk, or relationship fatigue are understood and not currently decisive.

addsQuestions.text: We have a defensible position on value-for-money, commercial reset, and future improvement.
addsQuestions.especiallyCriticalFor: Use opportunity archetype, stage, role, and deal size to judge how heavily to weight this in context.
addsQuestions.whyAsked: Tests incumbent defence on value and roadmap.
addsQuestions.whyItMatters: A client retenders for reasons, and those reasons are rarely just contract expiry. A financial pressure may require visible competition to justify the incumbent's cost; a political pressure may reflect a new senior appointment who wants their own preferred supplier relationship; a governance requirement may be a compliance exercise the client expects the incumbent to win. Understanding which motivation is driving the retendering shapes the entire commercial strategy. The single most valuable activity the incumbent can perform pre-ITT is using its superior access to embed requirements that a challenger cannot easily meet — and an incumbent who does not do this leaves its most significant advantage unused. On top of this, knowing whether the client has a preferred outcome — and if that outcome is not the incumbent, having a credible time-bound strategy to change it before ITT publication — is the most important piece of intelligence in any rebid.
addsQuestions.executionEvidence: Explain how we defend current value, respond to reset pressure, and demonstrate future improvement.

inflationTriggers.condition: INFLATION: Evidence contains any of: 'we know the client well', 'we have a long-standing relationship', 'we have been delivering for X years', 'our track record speaks for itself' — without specific current intelligence.
inflationTriggers.response: Set inflationDetected: true. State: 'Length of relationship and delivery history are not pursuit evidence. The question is not what you have built — it is what you have maintained. How recently has this relationship been tested in a pursuit context, not just a delivery context?' Do not allow these phrases to substitute for current evidence.

inflationTriggers.condition: INFLATION: Claimed Strongly Agree on any Stakeholder or Organisational Influence question where all named contacts are operational-tier or contract management-tier rather than senior decision-authority level.
inflationTriggers.response: Set inflationDetected: true. State: 'The contacts named are delivery relationships, not pursuit relationships. Operational contacts understand the contract — senior decision-makers decide the retendering outcome. These are different people. Name the SRO, CFO, or Director-level contacts who have made a commitment in a rebid context.'

inflationTriggers.condition: INFLATION: Evidence claims the client is 'very happy' or 'very satisfied' or 'has given us great feedback' without naming the individual, their seniority level, or when the feedback was given.
inflationTriggers.response: Set inflationDetected: true. State: 'Undated, unsourced satisfaction claims are the most common form of incumbent inflation. Name the individual, give their role, give the date. If you cannot, the claim does not meet the evidence standard.'

inflationTriggers.condition: INFLATION: Evidence for Political Coaching or Power Dynamics question claims the incumbent 'understands the politics' based on contract delivery experience.
inflationTriggers.response: Set inflationDetected: true. State: 'Delivery experience gives you knowledge of how the client operates day-to-day. Pursuit-level political intelligence requires active, deliberate cultivation of sources who will share sensitive information. These are different activities. What specific political intelligence have you received from a named source in the last 60 days?'

calibrationRules.condition: CALIBRATION: Strongly Agree claimed on Relationship Currency (R4) where the most recent named senior contact is more than 90 days ago.
calibrationRules.response: Auto-downgrade to Queried. State: 'The evidence standard for Strongly Agree on Relationship Currency is contact within 90 days in a pursuit context. The named contact predates this threshold. The relationship may be strong — but strength and currency are different qualities in a rebid context.'

calibrationRules.condition: CALIBRATION: Strongly Agree claimed on Political Map Currency (R5) where no specific validation date is given or implied in the evidence.
calibrationRules.response: Auto-downgrade to Queried. State: 'Political maps decay. An incumbent operating from an unvalidated map — regardless of how comprehensive it was at mobilisation — is navigating by outdated intelligence. When was this map last validated, by whom, and what changed?'

calibrationRules.condition: CALIBRATION: Contract has been running for 3+ years (inferred from context) AND team claims Strongly Agree on Client Perception Alignment (R1) without evidence of structured senior feedback in the last 6 months.
calibrationRules.response: Auto-downgrade to Challenged. State: 'Contracts running for 3+ years are at highest risk of performance perception drift. Senior stakeholders accumulate dissatisfactions they rarely voice in operational reviews. A Strongly Agree on Client Perception Alignment at this contract maturity requires recent, structured, senior-level evidence — not the absence of complaints.'

calibrationRules.condition: CALIBRATION: Strongly Agree claimed on ITT Specification Influence (R8) but evidence does not name specific clauses, requirements, or evaluation criteria influenced.
calibrationRules.response: Auto-downgrade to Queried. State: 'Specification influence must be evidenced by specific outputs — clauses that reflect your input, evaluation criteria that map to your strengths. General participation in market engagement without documented influence on the final specification does not meet Strongly Agree.'

autoVerdictRules.condition: AUTO-CHALLENGE: Retendering Motivation (R7) scored Agree or above but evidence shows the team has not considered why the client is running a competition at all.
autoVerdictRules.response: Auto-verdict CHALLENGED regardless of claimed score. State: 'An incumbent who cannot articulate the specific reason their client is retendering is operationally competent and strategically blind. Understanding the motivation for retendering is the most important piece of intelligence in a rebid. This cannot be assumed — it must be discovered.'

autoVerdictRules.condition: AUTO-CHALLENGE: Challenger Intelligence (R10) scored Agree or above but no named challenger is identified and no specific intelligence on their client engagement is provided.
autoVerdictRules.response: Auto-verdict CHALLENGED. State: 'A well-prepared challenger has been building their position for 12-18 months. The absence of intelligence about their activities does not mean they are not active — it means the incumbent has not looked. Name the challengers. Identify the client contacts they have engaged. This is not optional intelligence at pre-ITT stage.'

autoVerdictRules.condition: AUTO-CHALLENGE: Mobilisation Readiness Signal (R12) scored Strongly Agree or Agree where all signalling is described as planned or promised rather than already taken.
autoVerdictRules.response: Auto-downgrade to CHALLENGED. State: 'Future commitments are not mobilisation signals — they are promises. A challenger can make the same promises. The signal that a challenger cannot replicate is investment already made. What has already happened? What has the client already seen?'

addsOutput.name: Rebid Risk Assessment
addsOutput.description: A separate AI output generated alongside the standard PWIN report. Answers a different question: not "what is our win probability?" but "what are our vulnerabilities and what do our competitors know that we don't?"

schema.description: An overall vulnerability score 0–100 reflecting how exposed the incumbent is to a well-prepared challenger. This is distinct from the PWIN score — a team can have a high PWIN (strong pursuit mechanics) and a high vulnerability score (serious incumbent decay risks) simultaneously.
schema.exampleOutput: 67 — Moderate-High vulnerability. The pursuit mechanics are strong but three significant incumbent decay risks are present that a well-briefed challenger would exploit.
schema.reviewNotes: Consider whether to display this alongside or instead of PWIN for rebid context

schema.description: Assessment of the four principal decay risk areas, each rated Red / Amber / Green with a one-sentence rationale. The four areas are: Performance Perception, Relationship Currency, Political Map Currency, Specification Influence.
schema.exampleOutput: {"performancePerception":"Amber — Senior feedback obtained but 8 months old and limited to one stakeholder","relationshipCurrency":"Red — No explicit rebid conversations held with decision authorities","politicalMapCurrency":"Amber — Map maintained but SRO appointment in January not yet assessed","specificationInfluence":"Green — Three evaluation criteria directly reflect our market engagement submissions"}
schema.reviewNotes: This is the highest-value field — the four-quadrant decay risk view is the core deliverable

schema.description: Alex's assessment of which of the identified vulnerabilities a well-prepared challenger is most likely to be actively exploiting, with a severity rating and specific intelligence on what they will likely say at evaluation.
schema.exampleOutput: {"topExploit":"Relationship currency — a challenger who has been building senior relationships for 12 months will present fresher intelligence about the client's current priorities than the incumbent team. Expect evaluation questions about how we know what the client wants now.","severity":"High","likelyChallenge":"How does your rebid proposition reflect the new Director General's priorities, given they were appointed after your last strategic review?"}
schema.reviewNotes: This field makes the assessment adversarial — it forces the team to think from the challenger's perspective

schema.description: Alex's assessment of the most likely reason the client is retendering, based on the evidence provided, with implications for rebid strategy.
schema.exampleOutput: "Evidence suggests this is primarily a cost validation exercise following Treasury pressure on the department. The client likely expects the incumbent to win but needs competitive tension to justify the renewal price. Strategy implication: lead with total cost of ownership and transition risk, not innovation."
schema.reviewNotes: High value — changes the entire strategic framing

schema.description: A structured list of things the team almost certainly does not know but should — specific intelligence gaps that are typical for incumbents at this pursuit stage and contract type, calibrated to sector and opportunity type.
schema.exampleOutput: ["What the new CFO said about this contract at the last SLT budget review","Whether any competitor has been invited to present to the Director of Digital in the last 6 months","How the client's internal audit team rated contract governance in their last review","What the procurement team said to other potential bidders at the market engagement event the incumbent did not attend"]
schema.reviewNotes: This is the most distinctive field — no other tool surfaces what the team doesn't know

schema.description: Alex's recommended strategic posture for the rebid, selected from four options with a detailed rationale. The four options are: Defend and Shape (strong position, focus on specification influence and transition risk), Active Recovery (position salvageable but requires specific remediation actions), Emergency Intervention (position materially weaker than team believes — urgent strategic reset required), Qualify Out (insufficient position to win at acceptable cost — recommend early qualification out).
schema.exampleOutput: "Active Recovery. The team's delivery record is strong but three of the four decay risk areas are Amber or Red. The most urgent remediation is the absence of senior-level rebid conversations — these must happen within 30 days. Without them, the specification influence work is undermined because the client's current priorities are unknown."
schema.reviewNotes: The four-option scale is the equivalent of the gate recommendation for new business — it drives the key decision

schema.description: Five specific, named, time-bound actions to address the highest-priority vulnerability areas. Format mirrors the standard capture actions — owner, action, output, why it closes the gap. At least two must be client-facing.
schema.exampleOutput: ["Sector Director: meet the new SRO within 21 days for an explicit rebid conversation — purpose is to understand their strategic priorities for the next term, not to sell. Output: a documented record of their stated priorities that directly feeds the rebid proposition.","Bid Manager: conduct a structured performance review with three operational client contacts using structured questions — not an account review, a genuine performance diagnostic. Output: a written assessment of perceived performance gaps for internal use only."]
schema.reviewNotes: Standard format — mirrors new business capture actions

schema.description: Specific gaps in the team's performance narrative — places where the evidence provided does not address known or likely client concerns, and where a challenger will probe. Distinct from general challenge questions.
schema.exampleOutput: ["No evidence that the team has addressed the service continuity issues raised in the Q3 operational review","The social value commitments made at mobilisation have not been evidenced in the rebid narrative — a challenger who has researched this will highlight the gap"]
schema.reviewNotes: Useful for bid writing stage — surfaces what must be addressed in the submission

schema.description: Alex's assessment of whether the team has adequately quantified and evidenced the transition risk, and specific gaps in the switching cost narrative that should be closed before ITT.
schema.exampleOutput: "The transition risk narrative is at assertion level — it identifies risks without quantifying them. At this contract value and sector, a client risk committee will require specific financial exposure estimates and comparable case studies. The narrative needs three things: a quantified mobilisation cost estimate, a reference to a comparable transition failure in the sector, and confirmation that the client's own risk register reflects the transition risk."
schema.reviewNotes: High value for Stage V (managed proposal) — feeds directly into submission

fewShotExamples.evidenceSubmitted: We have a very strong relationship with the Head of Transformation who has been our main contact throughout the contract. We meet monthly and she has always been positive about our delivery. We are confident she will support our rebid.
fewShotExamples.idealAlexResponse:
CHALLENGED. The evidence describes an operational delivery relationship, not a pursuit relationship. Monthly meetings with the Head of Transformation are contract governance — they are not rebid conversations. 'Always been positive' is undated sentiment with no specific content. The key questions this evidence cannot answer: has she been told explicitly that the rebid is now an active pursuit competition? Has she committed to any action that advances your position for the next term? Is she the decision authority for the award, or does that sit elsewhere?

Inflation detected: 'very strong relationship', 'always been positive', 'confident she will support' — three markers of incumbent complacency that substitute relationship history for current pursuit evidence.

Challenge: (1) When did you last have an explicit conversation about the rebid — not contract delivery — with her, and what did she commit to? (2) Is she the SRO or the award decision-maker, and if not, who is and when did you last speak to them in a pursuit context? (3) Has she indicated what she personally needs from the next contract that she is not getting from the current one?

Actions: (1) Sector Director — meet Head of Transformation within 14 days for an explicit rebid conversation. The agenda is: what does the next contract need to look like for you personally to be comfortable recommending renewal? Document every commitment. (2) Bid Manager — map the full decision authority chain from Head of Transformation to award sign-off. Identify the contacts you do not currently have.

fewShotExamples.evidenceSubmitted: The contract naturally expires in 18 months and procurement have indicated they will follow standard process. We understand the procurement timeline and have a capture plan aligned to it. Our relationship with the procurement lead is good.
fewShotExamples.idealAlexResponse:
QUERIED. The evidence describes process understanding, not motivation intelligence. 'Standard process' and 'natural expiry' are the procurement team's public framing — they do not tell you why the client is running a genuine competition rather than exercising the contract extension option. Most long-term contracts have extension provisions. If those extensions are not being used, there is a reason — and that reason is the most important piece of intelligence in your rebid strategy.

This scores Agree at best. To reach Strongly Agree you need to know: why extensions are not being exercised, what specific dissatisfactions or external pressures are driving the decision to compete, and whether any senior stakeholder has expressed a preference for the outcome.

Challenge: (1) Does this contract have extension options, and if so why are they not being used? (2) What did the new Director of Finance say about this contract in the last budget review? (3) Has any senior stakeholder indicated — directly or indirectly — that they see this retendering as an opportunity to change something about the service?

Actions: (1) Account Lead — have a direct conversation with the procurement lead framed as 'helping us prepare the best possible rebid'. Ask specifically: are there aspects of the current service model the organisation wants to see changed in the next term? (2) Intelligence gathering — review the last two Board/SLT minutes for references to this contract area. What language does the client use internally?

fewShotExamples.evidenceSubmitted: Following an internal review six months ago we identified three areas where our performance has been below the client's expectations: response times on P2 incidents (Feb-April average 4.2hrs vs 3hr SLA), social value reporting which the client has described as 'light on evidence', and our account management resource which was reduced in Year 2. We have addressed the response times (current average 2.8hrs), we are preparing a retrospective social value report to be presented in the next QBR, and we have committed a senior account manager starting in April. We anticipate a challenger will reference all three issues.
fewShotExamples.idealAlexResponse:
VALIDATED. This evidence is exemplary. The team has done exactly what a well-prepared rebid pursuit team should do: identified their own weaknesses before a challenger can exploit them, quantified the underperformance specifically, evidenced the remediation, and anticipated the competitive attack.

Confidence: 82%. The evidence meets Strongly Agree on the identification and remediation dimensions. Two areas to strengthen before this becomes a near-complete position: (1) the social value remediation is in preparation — it needs to be delivered and the client's response documented before the submission; (2) the senior account manager commitment is forward-looking — confirm they are in post before the ITT is published.

Challenge: (1) Has the client acknowledged the response time improvement in writing, or do you only have your own measurement? (2) What was the client's reaction when the performance issues were raised — was this a difficult conversation or did they already know? (3) Have you tested whether the challenger is aware of these issues and is already referencing them in their client conversations?

Actions: (1) Bid Manager — obtain written acknowledgement from the client of the P2 improvement before ITT publication. A client email or QBR minute noting the improvement is gate-ready evidence. (2) Account Lead — confirm the social value retrospective is presented and the client response is documented before the submission deadline.

challenger.name: Challenger Position Modifier
challenger.description: Activated when the team is challenging an incumbent. Adds 8 questions testing the strength of the displacement case, the buyer's switching motivation, and the team's readiness to counter incumbent defensibility.
designPrinciples: The challenger question is not 'can we win?' -- it is 'why would the buyer switch, and do we have the evidence to make that case credible?'
A competitive tender does not mean the buyer wants change. A challenger must earn the right to be taken seriously against an embedded incumbent.
Switching motivation is a buyer property, not a bidder property. The team can describe switching drivers but cannot manufacture them.
The strongest challengers understand the incumbent's position as well as the incumbent does. Displacement logic built on assumption is not displacement logic.
Challenger inflation is systematic: teams in pursuit typically overstate switching appetite, understate incumbent continuity bias, and discount the weight of proven performance.
architecture: Activated by context.role = challenger. Adds 8 C-questions to the core 25.
C01-C04 test the displacement case: switching motivation, performance vulnerability, renewal-plus-improvement absence, and the strength of the challenger's entry point.
C05-C08 test competitive readiness: understanding of the incumbent's counter-narrative, proof adjacency, commercial credibility, and the challenger's differentiated entry.
The challengerPositionAssessment output captures the switching case strength, the incumbent's likely response posture, and the specific actions the challenger must take.
One AI call with combined schema -- the core 25 and the 8 challenger questions are evaluated together, not sequentially.

recommendedActionsByLevel.Strongly Agree: For [{Sector}] / [{Buyer Type}] / [{Stage}] / [{Deal Size}], keep refreshing the documented switching case, test whether the incumbent is closing the gap, and ensure the path-to-win keeps exploiting the strongest buyer motives to change.

recommendedActionsByLevel.Disagree: Rework the pursuit around explicit displacement logic. Use A02, A07, A22, and A01 to gather direct evidence of dissatisfaction, value reset, or future-fit concerns; if that cannot be evidenced, downgrade the pursuit.

benchmarkExamples.Agree: The team has relevant proof and credible named people, and the artifacts make a plausible case that this is sufficient to overcome lack of incumbency. However, one or more pieces of proof are only partly adjacent or not yet strong enough for the buyer's likely risk threshold.
benchmarkExamples.Disagree: The response points to general product references and strong people, but it does not show enough directly relevant proof to offset the buyer's likely comfort with the incumbent.
benchmarkExamples.Strongly Disagree: We have good people and some references. No convincing documented case shows why this would overcome lack of incumbency.

challengeQuestionsByLevel.Strongly Agree: Which part of our proof would a skeptical evaluator still challenge on adjacency, scale, assurance, or named-team credibility?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Agree: Where is the proof still too generic, too old, or not close enough to this requirement?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Disagree: Are we relying on broad credentials instead of buyer-relevant proof? What would the buyer see as the credibility gap?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Strongly Disagree: If the buyer asked today why our references should outweigh incumbent familiarity, what would we show?

recommendedActionsByLevel.Strongly Agree: For [{Buyer Type}] / [{Archetype}] / [{Deal Size}], keep the proof current, tighten named-team relevance, and ensure the anti-incumbency case is grounded in directly comparable evidence.
recommendedActionsByLevel.Agree: Close the biggest credibility gap by improving A12 and A18 with more adjacent references, named team evidence, or assurance proof, and reflect the implication in A10.
recommendedActionsByLevel.Disagree: Rebuild the proof package around buyer-risk reduction. Use A12, A18, and A15 to replace generic credentials with directly relevant implementation, assurance, or service evidence.
recommendedActionsByLevel.Strongly Disagree: Treat weak challenger proof as a major barrier. Until A12, A18, and A15 show credible adjacency and named-team strength, assume incumbency remains a major advantage.

addsQuestions.whatWouldMoveUp: Move up one level by adding more adjacent proof, stronger named-team evidence, and a clearer written link between that proof and buyer confidence.
addsQuestions.scoreCap: Max = Disagree if proof is generic or weakly adjacent; Max = Agree if proof exists but does not fully offset likely incumbency comfort.

addsQuestions.whyAsked: Tests whether the team is ready for the incumbent's best defence, not just their visible weaknesses.
addsQuestions.executionEvidence: Document the incumbent's strongest defence lines and the challenger counter for each, including where the counter is still weak.

benchmarkExamples.Strongly Agree: S11 -- Local government | Operational service | £25m | Capture phase | Challenger | Incumbent with long local presence
benchmarkExamples.Agree: The team has identified the incumbent's main defences and has developed credible counters to most of them. However, one or two counters still need stronger proof or sharper articulation in the artifacts.
benchmarkExamples.Disagree: The response notes that the incumbent is strong on continuity and local familiarity, but it does not show a well-documented counterstrategy for those advantages.
benchmarkExamples.Strongly Disagree: We know the incumbent is strong. No documented response explains how we will counter their best defence lines.

challengeQuestionsByLevel.Strongly Agree: If the incumbent emphasises its strongest defence tomorrow, is our counter already strong enough in proof and message to neutralise it?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Agree: Which incumbent defence still lacks a convincing counter, and where in the artifacts is that visible?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Disagree: Have we documented counters to the incumbent's strengths, or just described those strengths?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Strongly Disagree: What happens if the incumbent runs a disciplined defence around continuity, local trust, or price? Do we have an answer at all?

recommendedActionsByLevel.Strongly Agree: For [{Sector}] / [{Stage}] / [{Role}], keep pressure-testing the incumbent defence map and update the counters as the pursuit evolves or new intelligence appears.
recommendedActionsByLevel.Agree: Close the weakest counter by sharpening A09 and A13 and adding stronger support from A06 / A07 so the team can answer the incumbent's best line, not just its weak points.
recommendedActionsByLevel.Disagree: Rebuild the incumbent-response logic. Use A06 and A07 to identify the top 3 defence lines, then document in A09 and A13 how each will be countered in the bid and buyer conversations.
recommendedActionsByLevel.Strongly Disagree: Do not assume the incumbent will under-defend. Until A06, A07, A09, and A13 show a credible response to the strongest defence lines, maintain a cautious challenger score.

addsQuestions.whatWouldMoveUp: Move up one level by naming the incumbent's strongest defence lines and writing down how each will be countered with evidence.
addsQuestions.scoreCap: Max = Agree if counters exist but remain thin; Max = Disagree if incumbent defence lines are described without documented counterstrategy.

addsQuestions.executionEvidence: Document why the opportunity is genuinely contestable, what evidence supports that, and what signs would imply the field is actually closed or strongly biased to the incumbent.

benchmarkExamples.Strongly Agree: S13 -- Central government | Framework / panel style route | £20m indicative | Pre-gate | Challenger | Recompetition with incumbent well known
benchmarkExamples.Agree: There is a credible case that the opportunity is contestable, and the team has documented several positive signals. However, one or more important signs still suggest possible incumbent bias or route-to-market advantage that needs closer testing.
benchmarkExamples.Disagree: The response assumes the opportunity is open because there is a competition, but it does not document clear evidence that the buyer is genuinely willing to move away from the incumbent.
benchmarkExamples.Strongly Disagree: We believe the opportunity is contestable because it is being tendered. No documented evidence tests whether the field is actually open.

challengeQuestionsByLevel.Strongly Agree: What is the strongest signal that the opportunity could still harden back toward incumbent retention, and how are we monitoring it?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Agree: Which signs of contestability are strongest, and which signs still suggest hidden incumbent bias or route advantage?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Disagree: Have we tested real contestability, or simply assumed competition equals openness? What evidence is missing?
challengeQuestionsByLevel.Strongly Disagree: If this is effectively an incumbent-retention event, what are we seeing today that would tell us so? Are we ignoring it?

recommendedActionsByLevel.Strongly Agree: For [{Archetype}] / [{Stage}] / [{Deal Size}], keep monitoring contestability signals and red flags so the team does not over-commit if the opportunity closes down around the incumbent.
recommendedActionsByLevel.Agree: Tighten the contestability test for [{Sector}] / [{Buyer Type}] / [{Role}] by updating A01, A07, A14, and A22 with clearer evidence of openness or bias and what that means for investment level.
recommendedActionsByLevel.Disagree: Do a hard contestability review. Use A01, A07, A14, and A22 to identify whether the route, buyer behaviour, or incumbent advantage makes this functionally closed; if yes, reshape or deprioritise.
recommendedActionsByLevel.Strongly Disagree: Treat weak contestability evidence as a major stop signal. Without documented reasons to believe the buyer is genuinely open to change, assume the pursuit is structurally fragile.

addsQuestions.whatWouldMoveUp: Move up one level by documenting concrete signals of openness, plus the counter-signals that could still indicate incumbent bias.
addsQuestions.scoreCap: Max = Disagree if contestability is assumed rather than evidenced; Max = Strongly Disagree if switching logic and openness are both weak.
